ending: The Corpsetaker is destroyed for good: her assault on Mortimer Lindquist ends when Mort, far stronger than he ever let anyone see, turns his mastery of spirits against her and unmakes her ghost, with Sir Stuart spent in his defense, Murphy's crew storming the physical battlefield, and Bob checkmating his dark twin in the Nevernever. Harry's murder is solved, and the answer was always going to hurt: he arranged it himself - he hired Kincaid to shoot him and had Molly erase his memory of the plan, choosing death over life as Mab's corrupted Winter Knight, and unknowingly leaving Molly to carry the secret alone into breakdown. Uriel shows Harry the rest: his despairing choice was not entirely free - a fallen angel whispered to him at his lowest moment, and Uriel, permitted to balance the scales, grants Harry seven words in return: the whisper was a lie, and Mab cannot change who he is - only Harry can do that. Then the final truth: Harry was never wholly dead. His body has lain preserved on Demonreach, kept alive by Mab and the island, while his spirit walked. Harry chooses to go back. He wakes in the flesh, weak but alive, with Mab attending his recovery and his service as the Winter Knight still owed. Chicago's defenders do not yet know he is coming back; Maggie remains safe with the Carpenters; and the Fomor remain loose in the world.
in_short: Shot dead at the end of Changes, Harry Dresden is sent back to Chicago as a ghost to find his murderer, warned that three of his friends will suffer if he fails. Six months have passed: the Fomor have risen to fill the Red Court's vacuum, Murphy leads the city's ragged supernatural resistance, Butters carries Bob's skull, and Molly has broken into the murderous vigilante called the Rag Lady. Unable to touch or be seen, Harry allies with the ectomancer Mortimer Lindquist and the shade of Sir Stuart, whose home is besieged nightly by the Grey Ghost - the Corpsetaker, the body-stealing necromancer Harry once killed, now hunting a new body with Mort's at the top of her list. While steering a talented street kid named Fitz out of a petty sorcerer's grip, Harry recovers his own missing memories and solves the case: he arranged his own murder, hiring Kincaid and having Molly erase the memory, rather than live as Mab's monster. Mort destroys the Corpsetaker, the archangel Uriel reveals a fallen angel's whisper tainted Harry's fatal choice - and that his body has been kept alive on Demonreach by Mab and the island all along. Harry chooses to return, and wakes: alive, and still the Winter Knight.

recaps.text: Previously in the series: Harry Dresden, Chicago's professional wizard, learned he had a daughter - eight-year-old Maggie, hidden from him by his old love Susan Rodriguez - when the Red Court of vampires took the girl for a ritual sacrifice designed to kill Harry's entire bloodline. Stripped of his office, his home, and finally the use of his legs, Harry bought the power to save her at the price he had refused for years: he became the Winter Knight, mortal champion of Mab, Queen of Air and Darkness. At Chichen Itza his raiders shattered the Red Court's ritual; the traitor Martin engineered his own death at Susan's hands, turning her into a full vampire, and Harry killed her on the altar, hijacking the bloodline curse to annihilate the entire Red Court worldwide. He learned in the same battle that his old mentor Ebenezar McCoy is his grandfather. Maggie was placed safely with the Carpenter family, guarded by Mouse. Then, hours later, before he could begin his service to Mab, a sniper's bullet took Harry in the chest on the deck of his brother's boat, and he fell into Lake Michigan and died. His murderer is unknown. He leaves behind Karrin Murphy, his apprentice Molly Carpenter, Butters, Bob the skull, his half-brother Thomas Raith, and a city that no longer has a wizard to protect it.

Harry wakes from his death in a city that is not quite anywhere - a rain-swept, out-of-time Chicago run like a police precinct - where Captain Jack Murphy, Karrin Murphy's long-dead father, hands him a choice dressed as an assignment: go back as a spirit and find your murderer, or three of the people you love will come to harm. Harry goes back, and lands in a Chicago six months after his death with almost nothing he counted on. He is a ghost: insubstantial, invisible and inaudible to nearly everyone, cut off from his magic, endangered by sunrise, and made - as he quickly learns - of memory rather than matter. The only door open to him is Mortimer Lindquist, Chicago's professional ectomancer, who can see and hear the dead and wants absolutely nothing to do with a dead wizard's problems. Mort's household is guarded by the shade of Sir Stuart, a Colonial marine with centuries of discipline, and it needs guarding: every night, mad and hungry spirits press an organized siege against Mort's defenses, directed by a grey-cloaked ghost with a necromancer's control of the dead. Reluctantly, Mort agrees to help Harry make contact with the living - starting with the people who used to be his friends.

Harry gets his first full look at what his death cost. With the Red Court annihilated, new predators - the Fomor, risen from beneath the sea - have moved into the vacuum, snatching practitioners off the streets of every major city, and Chicago's defense has fallen to a scraped-together alliance led by Karrin Murphy: werewolves, minor talents, and ordinary people, organized and grim. Mort, speaking for a ghost no one can see, has to convince a room full of Harry's grieving friends that Harry's shade is real and not another trick - Butters, now the group's supernatural analyst and keeper of Bob's skull, applies actual rigor to the question, and Bob can vouch for his old boss. The city's nights hold a new terror that even the Fomor's servitors fear: the Rag Lady, a half-mad guerrilla who leaves corpses marked with rag dolls - and Harry learns she is Molly, his apprentice, broken by his death and fighting a filthy war alone. Meanwhile Harry crosses a crew of homeless teenage practitioners under a petty sorcerer called Aristedes; one of them, a boy named Fitz, has enough talent to hear a ghost's voice. Unable to touch, spell, or comfort anyone, Harry starts doing what he can with what he has: words.

Being dead turns out to have rules, and Harry learns them the hard way. A ghost is memory given shape: his power and his self are both made of what he remembers, and between the events of the present he keeps dropping into vivid recollections of his own past - his brutal apprenticeship under Justin DuMorne, his early days in Chicago - each one a piece of the puzzle of who killed him and why he was sent back. He works the Fitz problem like the caseworker he never got to be: talking the boy through fear and loyalty, steering him and his crew away from Aristedes and toward Father Forthill's protection, paying forward the rescue Harry himself once needed. The siege of Mort's home escalates from probing attacks to a full assault - lemurs and wraiths spent like ammunition by the grey-cloaked spectre, Sir Stuart grievously diminished holding the line - and it ends in defeat: Mortimer Lindquist is taken. The enemy's purpose comes into focus. The Grey Ghost is hunting a body, and a body with an ectomancer's gifts is the finest prize in Chicago. Harry, guilt-ridden and running out of night, understands that whoever is behind the siege planned for the city's ghosts and specialists alike - and that he has seen this playbook before.

The enemy has a name Harry hoped never to hear again: the Corpsetaker. One of the heirs of the necromancer Kemmler, a body-thief who wore other people's flesh for centuries, she was killed in the flesh years ago during the necromancer summer of Dead Beat - but a spirit that strong does not simply end, and her ghost has been rebuilding power in Chicago's underworld ever since. Now she holds Mort, and means to break his will and take his body, returning to the living world with an ectomancer's authority over the dead layered on her own necromancy. Her resources run deeper than wraiths: her Nevernever stronghold is garrisoned by mad spirits and overseen by a being Harry's allies call Evil Bob - a dark fragment of Bob the skull, split off in service to Kemmler's heirs, with all of Bob's genius and none of his loyalty. Harry scouts, plans, and recruits: Sir Stuart's remnant, the ghosts of soldiers, and the living - Murphy's crew, Butters and Bob, and Molly, whom Harry finally reaches. The reunion cuts both ways: her guilt and his are tangled together in ways Harry does not yet fully remember. With Mort weakening by the hour, the rescue cannot wait for Harry to finish solving his own murder.

The assault comes together on two fronts. In the material world, Murphy's people - werewolves, guns, and Butters' methodical planning - move on the Corpsetaker's mortal foothold, where her captive and her cultists are hidden. In the spirit world, Harry leads the dead: Sir Stuart's soldiers, marshaled for one more campaign, against the stronghold's garrison of mad ghosts. Butters brings the decisive asymmetry - Bob, carried into battle against his own dark twin, spirit of intellect against spirit of intellect, the one contest Evil Bob cannot delegate. The fighting strips Harry down to essentials, and in the middle of it the last of his missing memories begin to surface: the black weeks after Chichen Itza, the certainty of what life as Mab's Winter Knight would make of him, and a plan - his own voice, in his own memory, arranging something he chose to forget. Harry starts to understand that the question Captain Murphy sent him back to answer is shaped very differently than he assumed, and that the answer has been walking around Chicago wearing his own face. Ahead of him wait the Corpsetaker, at the peak of her gathered power, and a truth about the night he died that implicates the person he least wants it to.

The battle and the case break open together. In the stronghold, the Corpsetaker's assault on Mort's will reaches its endgame - and fails, catastrophically for her, because Mortimer Lindquist was never the timid fraud he played. With his defenders buying him the moment he needs, Mort turns the full, hidden weight of his ectomancy against the Corpsetaker's naked spirit and unmakes her, ending Kemmler's disciple permanently. Sir Stuart is spent in the victory; Bob outplays his dark twin; Murphy's crew sweeps the mortal field. And Harry, his memories restored, faces the answer he was sent back for: no one murdered Harry Dresden except Harry Dresden. Facing life as the Winter Knight - certain the mantle would slowly turn him into a monster wearing his face, with his daughter in the world to see it - he contracted his own assassination, hiring Kincaid, the one shooter who could beat him, and had Molly erase his memory of the arrangement so that not even Mab could see it coming. Molly has carried that secret, alone, through six months of breakdown and guerrilla war. The case is closed, the three friends are safe - and what remains is the accounting for a choice that Harry made, but that may not have been entirely his own.

The accounting arrives in the person of Uriel. The archangel - whose business is free will, and whose people have been quietly involved since the between-place precinct where this all began - shows Harry the flaw in his fatal choice: in his blackest moment before his death, a fallen angel whispered despair into his ear, an uninvited thumb on the scale of his decision. Cheating of that kind permits a balancing response, and Uriel grants Harry exactly what the enemy spent: seven words - the whisper was a lie; Mab cannot change who Harry is. Only Harry can do that. Then the last veil comes off the whole affair: Harry was never entirely dead. The bullet dropped him into Lake Michigan, but his body has lain on Demonreach ever since, kept alive between Mab's power and the island's, while his spirit walked and learned what it needed to learn. Offered the choice that has underwritten everything - to move on, or to go back to a hard life, a waiting queen, and a war-torn city - Harry chooses to go back. He wakes in his own flesh, wasted and weak, with Mab herself presiding over his recovery and his service as the Winter Knight still to begin. Molly no longer carries the secret alone; Maggie is safe with the Carpenters; the Fomor still hold the world's coastlines; and Chicago is about to get its wizard back.
